paper: Pérez Pérez (2020), The Minimum Wage in Formal and Informal Sectors: Evidence from an Inflation Shock
q_ranking: Q1 by current SCImago/Scopus-based ranking; final library verification recommended
data: Colombia National Household Survey (ENH), minimum wage data, CPI, financial sector GDP
Y: Formal wages, informal wages, formal employment, informal employment, hours
D: Minimum wage incidence by city-industry block interacted with post-1998q4 shock
W: City-industry FE, time FE, city trends, industry trends, Bartik shocks, financial sector GDP
method: Difference-in-Differences; RIF/unconditional quantile regression; continuous treatment intensity
identification: Unexpected 1999 real minimum wage increase from inflation forecast error; compares high- vs low-incidence city-industry blocks
result: Wages near minimum rise in both sectors; larger formal wage response; slight negative informal employment effects; no clear formal employment loss
limitation: Colombia context; shock coincides with financial crisis; generalization to Vietnam limited; method is DiD/RIF-DiD not DML
dataset_access: Replication package linked on author website/Harvard Dataverse; local access pending
our_improvement: Use as main benchmark; if replication data accessible, reproduce DiD then add nonlinearity diagnostic and DML theta-stability robustness

paper: Nguyen Cuong Viet (2023/2025), The Impact of Minimum Wages on Employment: Evidence from a Lower Middle-Income Country
journal_or_source: The Developing Economies, vol. 63(2), 2025; GLO Discussion Paper No. 1253 in 2023
q_ranking: Q ranking needs final verification for The Developing Economies
data: Vietnam annual Labor Force Surveys and district minimum wage data
unit_of_observation: Individual workers/district-year or local labor-market panel
Y: Employment, monthly earnings, working hours, hourly earnings
W: Individual and local controls; district and province-year fixed effects
identification: Uses regional minimum wage variation over time in Vietnam
result: No significant overall employment/monthly earnings effects; reduced weekly hours; increased hourly earnings
limitation: Not centered on province-year informal_rate; focuses on wage workers and hours/wages
dataset_access: Published abstract and GLO paper accessible; microdata access needs checking
our_improvement: Use as Vietnam data/method benchmark; compare FE logic and recent Vietnam period

paper: Del Carpio et al. (2013/2018 MPRA), The Impact of Minimum Wages on Employment, Wages and Welfare: The Case of Vietnam
q_ranking: No journal/Q ranking assigned to MPRA version unless journal version is found
Y: Wage employment, informal contracts/formal jobs, self-employment, wages, welfare
D: Minimum wage changes/real minimum wage exposure
W: Worker, household, firm and location controls; province/district/firm fixed effects
method: OLS; province FE; district FE; firm FE
identification: Policy variation across Vietnam minimum wage regimes and time
result: Minimum wage changes reduce wage employment especially in domestic firms, increase self-employment, raise wages for job stayers and affect welfare
limitation: Older data; working paper version; not focused on province-year informal_rate; no DML
dataset_access: PDF available locally; replication data not in repo
our_improvement: Use as Vietnam context benchmark and reference for real minimum wage/FE construction

paper: Chernozhukov et al. (2018), Double/debiased machine learning for treatment and structural parameters
q_ranking: Q ranking not central for this project role
D: Treatment or policy variable in generic causal setting
method: Double/debiased machine learning; Neyman orthogonal score; cross-fitting
identification: Valid inference requires credible identifying assumptions plus nuisance estimation
result: DML reduces regularization/selection bias for causal parameters under assumptions
limitation: Not a minimum wage or informality paper; DML does not create exogenous variation
our_improvement: Use only for DML theory and theta-stability reporting; do not claim DML proves causality

paper: Comola and de Mello (2009), How does decentralized minimum wage setting affect employment and informality?
q_ranking: No journal/Q assigned to working-paper/report version
identification: Decentralized minimum wage variation across districts/time
result: Higher minimum-to-mean wage ratios associated with lower formal employment and higher informality
limitation: Endogeneity of local minimum wage setting; aggregate panel; Indonesia-specific institutions
our_improvement: Use as close developing-country informality benchmark; compare with province-year Vietnam panel

paper: Lemos (2009), Minimum wage effects in a developing country
Y: Wages and employment in formal/informal sectors
D: Minimum wage measures including fraction at/affected and wage ratios
method: Reduced-form equations with regional and time fixed effects
identification: Uses Brazilian minimum wage changes and regional variation
result: Evidence of wage-distribution responses and limited/mixed employment effects in developing-country context
limitation: Brazil institutions and inflation history differ from Vietnam; exact specification depends on version
dataset_access: Article/working paper information available; data access needs checking
our_improvement: Use to frame formal/informal spillovers and developing-country wage effects

paper: Dinkelman and Ranchhod (2012), Evidence on the impact of minimum wage laws in an informal sector: Domestic workers in South Africa
q_ranking: Usually Q1 in development economics; final year/category verification recommended
data: South African labor force/domestic worker data
period: Around domestic worker minimum wage law implementation in early 2000s
Y: Wages, employment, hours in domestic work
D: Introduction of minimum wage law for domestic workers
W: Worker/location controls and policy exposure controls
identification: Policy extension to a largely informal sector
result: Minimum wage law raised wages with limited employment effects in the domestic-worker sector
limitation: Sector-specific; domestic work differs from Vietnam aggregate informal employment
dataset_access: Article accessible; replication access needs checking
our_improvement: Use as informal-sector policy benchmark and careful external-validity example

paper: Cengiz et al. (2019), The effect of minimum wages on low-wage jobs
data: US Current Population Survey and 138 prominent state minimum wage changes
Y: Low-wage jobs, wages, employment by wage bins
identification: Event-study around many state minimum wage increases
result: Wage gains at bottom with little evidence of net low-wage job loss
limitation: Developed-country setting; no informal sector outcome
our_improvement: Use as distributional/event-study benchmark, not direct Vietnam comparison

paper: Card and Krueger (1994), Minimum Wages and Employment: A Case Study of the Fast-Food Industry in New Jersey and Pennsylvania
data: Survey of fast-food restaurants in New Jersey and Pennsylvania
D: New Jersey minimum wage increase relative to Pennsylvania
identification: Compares treated New Jersey to control Pennsylvania before/after increase
result: Finds no evidence of employment decline in this sector/context
limitation: Sector-specific; short-run survey; external validity debated; no informality
dataset_access: Published article; replication data varies by source
our_improvement: Use as classic DiD benchmark and motivation for control-group requirements

paper: Dube, Lester and Reich (2010), Minimum Wage Effects Across State Borders
q_ranking: Q1/top field journal; final category verification recommended
identification: Compares contiguous counties across state borders to reduce spatial confounding
result: Finds earnings effects and no adverse employment effects in low-wage sectors
limitation: US border design not directly transferable to Vietnam regional wage zones
our_improvement: Use to motivate spatial heterogeneity and province FE/local trend concerns

paper: Hohberg and Lay (2015), The impact of minimum wages on informal and formal labor market outcomes
data: Around Indonesian minimum wage changes; exact period needs verification
Y: Provincial minimum wages or minimum wage bite
W: Econometric policy evaluation with fixed effects
method: Uses Indonesian regional minimum wage variation
identification: Finds limited spillovers to informal sector in some specifications
result: Details require full-text verification; Indonesia-specific institutions
dataset_access: Use as developing-country formal/informal benchmark once verified

paper: Maloney and Nunez (2004), Measuring the impact of minimum wages: evidence from Latin America
journal_or_source: World Bank/University of Chicago volume; no journal Q assigned
q_ranking: Household/labor data from Latin American countries
Y: Minimum wage levels relative to wages
identification: Shows minimum wages can affect wage distributions beyond the formal sector/lighthouse effects
result: Not Vietnam-specific; exact specifications need verification
dataset_access: Use for framing lighthouse/reference-wage effects in informal markets
